items.label: About ZYNQ.peer
items.content:
# ZYNQ.peer

`ZYNQ.peer` is a lightweight and user-friendly WebRTC wrapper built on top of PeerJS. It makes real-time peer-to-peer communication (messaging, audio and video) much simpler.

---

## Core Features

- Easy peer-to-peer messaging (one-to-one + broadcast)
- Audio & video calls with simple configuration
- Automatic invite / accept flow for media
- Built-in heartbeat and timeout system
- Automatic local stream management and cleanup
- Bitrate monitoring
- Clean event-driven API
- Zero backend required

items.label: Starting the Peer
items.content:
# Starting the Peer

```javascript
await ZYNQ.peer.start([customId])
```

- `customId` (optional): Your own peer ID (string). Leave empty for a random ID generated by PeerJS.
- This connects you to the PeerJS broker server.
- When successful, it fires the `'ready'` event with your ID.

items.label: Destroy the Peer
items.content:
# Destroy everything

```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.destroy()
```

Closes all connections, stops local camera/microphone, clears heartbeats and destroys the PeerJS instance. Emits the `'destroyed'` event.

items.label: All Events
items.content:
# Events

You can listen to events with:
```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.on('event-name', data => {
  // handle event
})
```

## Complete list of events

| Event | When it fires | What you receive | Typical usage |
|-------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| `ready` | When your peer is connected to the broker | `id` (string) – your own peer ID | Display your ID |
| `invite` | When someone wants to connect to you | `{ id: string, config: object, accept: function, reject: function }` | Accept or reject incoming call |
| `joined` | When a connection is successfully established | `peerId` (string) | Update UI, show call interface |
| `left` | When a peer disconnects | `peerId` (string) | Remove video element, clean UI |
| `message` | When you receive a text message | `{ peerId: string, message: any }` | Display chat message |
| `local-stream` | When your own camera/microphone stream is ready | `MediaStream` object | Set local video preview: `localVideo.srcObject = stream` |
| `remote-stream` | When you receive video/audio from another peer | `{ peerId: string, stream: MediaStream }` | Set remote video: `remoteVideo.srcObject = stream` |
| `bitrate` | Every ~2 seconds with video stats | `{ peerId: string, kbps: number }` | Show connection quality |
| `rejected` | When the other peer rejects your connection | `peerId` (string) | Show “Call rejected” message |
| `error` | When something goes wrong | `{ code: string, peerId?: string, message: string }` | Log or show error to user |
| `destroyed` | After calling `destroy()` | (no data) | Clean up UI completely |

### Detailed explanation of each event

**`ready`** Fired when your peer successfully connects to the PeerJS broker. You receive your own peer ID as a string.

**`invite`** Fired when another peer calls `connect()` on you. You receive an object containing: `id`, `config`, `accept()` and `reject()`. Example: `ZYNQ.peer.on('invite', (data) => { console.log('Invite from:', data.id); data.accept(); })`

**`joined`** Fired after you accept an invite or after your own `connect()` is accepted. You receive the `peerId`.

**`left`** Fired when a peer disconnects (manually or by timeout). You receive the `peerId`.

**`message`** Fired when a text message arrives. You receive `{ peerId, message }`. The message can be any type.

**`local-stream`** Fired when `getUserMedia` succeeds for your own camera/microphone. You receive the raw `MediaStream` object.

**`remote-stream`** Fired when the other peer sends their video/audio stream. You receive `{ peerId, stream }`.

**`bitrate`** Fired approximately every 2 seconds when video is active. You receive `{ peerId, kbps }`.

**`rejected`** Fired when the remote peer calls `reject()` on your invite. You receive the `peerId`.

**`error`** Fired on various errors. You receive an object with `code`, optional `peerId`, and `message`.

**`destroyed`** Fired after `ZYNQ.peer.destroy()` completes.

items.label: Messaging
items.content:
# Messaging

## Send a message to one peer
```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.sendTo(peerId, message)
```
`message` can be a string, number, object or array.

## Send to all connected peers
```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.broadcast(message)
```

## Receiving messages
```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.on('message', ({ peerId, message }) => {
  console.log(`From ${peerId}:`, message)
})
```

items.label: Video & Audio Calls
Managing Connections
items.content:
# Connection Management

## Disconnect a specific peer
```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.disconnect(peerId)
```

## Get your own ID
```javascript
const myId = ZYNQ.peer.getMyId()
```

## Get list of currently connected peers
```javascript
const activePeers = ZYNQ.peer.getActivePeers() // array of strings
```

items.label: System Behavior & Advanced Options
items.content:
# System Behavior

- **Heartbeat**: Automatically sends a ping every 2 seconds
- **Timeout**: Disconnects a peer after 12 seconds of inactivity
- **Local stream**: Reused across calls and automatically stopped when no peers are connected
- **Bitrate monitoring**: Only active with video, emits the `bitrate` event

## Advanced settings

### Debug level
```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.setDebug(2) // 0 = off, 1 = normal, 2+ = detailed logging
```

### Disable auto-start
```javascript
ZYNQ.peer.setAutoStart(false)
```
